prediction.summary: A credential stuffing attack targeted the 'user@corp' account across both SSH and a web application. Multiple failed login attempts from various source IPs were observed, followed by a successful login for 'user@corp' from IP 198.51.100.123. An audit system confirmed distributed credential reuse, indicating a successful account compromise.

timeline.event: Failed SSH password for user@corp from 198.51.100.12
Failed SSH password for user@corp from 198.51.100.33
Failed SSH password for user@corp from 198.51.100.44
Failed SSH password for user@corp from 198.51.100.88
Failed SSH password for user@corp from 198.51.100.101
Accepted SSH password for user@corp from 198.51.100.123
Distributed credential reuse detected across 8 source IPs for account user@corp

recommendations: Immediately block the source IP 198.51.100.123 and other identified attacking IPs (e.g., 198.51.100.12, .33, .44, .55, .66, .77, .88, .99, .101, .112) at the firewall or security group.
Disable the compromised account 'user@corp' and force a password reset for the user. Ensure the new password meets strong complexity requirements.
Investigate post-login activity on the 'web' host for 'user@corp' to identify any further malicious actions, command execution, or lateral movement.
Review Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) logs for 'user@corp' to determine if MFA was bypassed or not in use for the successful login.
Correlate with Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) alerts and other security logs on the affected host for additional indicators of compromise.
Review logs for other user accounts for similar distributed login attempts or successful logins following failed attempts, indicating a broader credential stuffing campaign.
